## Analysis of UK infectious disease modelling network
### RCT  
- [https://trestlelink.org/models/research-to-policy-collaboration/](https://trestlelink.org/models/research-to-policy-collaboration/)   
- [https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2012955118](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2012955118)  
- 
### Dynamics of UK outbreak modelling expertise

What is the shape of UK outbreak modelling?
- Social network analysis

Rationale:

_Operational interest_
In a short term crisis, we need to mobilist expertise quickly
A core critique of the UK's advisory infrastructure was groupthink: access to a wider pool of expertise would help address this
However modelling expertise is difficult to identify: modellers are traditionally methodological contributors to a domain team and data on modelling work is not specifically/sensitively indexed in e.g. MeSH subheadings
- RQ1: test the sensitivity and specificity of citation network analysis as a tool for identifying the population of UK academic outbreak modelling work
- RQ2: identify blind spots / hot spots in UK modelling (vs outsourcing expertise?) against potential future response needs (eg national risk register). How does UK modelling expertise align with priorities?

_Scientific interest_
Infectious disease epi is shaped by crisis response; expert advisors are shaped by the crisis they respond to
Qualitatively strong survivor bias in oubtreak response expertise (cumulative interest in the citations of early response papers, disproportionate early exits / persistent players in the field)
First outbreak response also forms the anchor of future work and a template for how they respond in future which may be more or less appropriate
Specifically the public health response to an outbreak is not typically within the advisor's control, but still shapes their expectations of future advice
Example outbreaks: HIV, FMD, SARS, H1N1, Ebola
- RQ3: test for the "matthew effect" (cumulative power): early response disproportionately increasing weight/centrality in the network
- RQ4: identify how the patterns of one oubtreak influenced response to subsequent

#### Methods

_Data collection_
- Identify citation network
  - Start with COVID as single event all modelling authors will likely have interacted with
  - Population: papers modelling COVID with at least one author with UK institutional affiliation
  - Citation network: backward (who they trained with) and forward (who they are training)
- Map papers to time, outbreak response, modelling method
_Data analysis_
RQ1: 
- Identify clusters of domain/method/author
- Follow up verification via survey across entire network
RQ2: 
- Compare clusters against national priorities
RQ3: 
- Stratify authors by working on one vs multiple outbreaks
- look at some kind of expected vs observed citation count based on adjusted for timing during outbreak; see if this carries through into later work
- (stratified) random sample for survey/interview follow up

Limitations:
- Doesn't capture outbreak modellers who chose not to interact with COVID
- Less likely to capture outbreak modellers of non-human pathogens

#### Results

Outputs: 
- density/dispersion of network
  - persistence
- waves of domain expertise over time
  - average across network
  - moving across institutions

#### Interpretation

Outcomes:
- understand how landscape of expertise is shaped by crisis events
-
